![](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6B-6kWPIK_8)

Applicable Models:

- **Project Time:** 1 hour
- **Tab:** $1600
- **Talent**
- **Tools:** [Trim removal tool](/Mercedes-Benz/catalog/tools/body-shop.htm), T20 [Torx](/Mercedes-Benz/catalog/tools/torx-sockets-bits.htm)

- **Parts Required:**
- **Performance Gain:** Working instrument
- **Complementary Modification:** Detail the interior

## The instrument cluster on the Mercedes W204 is a single sealed unit with a single wiring connection on the back of it.

While complete diagnosis of problems with the cluster are beyond the scope of this article it will show you how to remove the console if you need to replace it or send it out for repairs.

Hot tip

Remove the cluster carefully

Steve Vernon, DIY Expert

//

## Project Photos

Figure 1 Begin by moving the steering wheel as far back (yellow arrow) and as low as it will go (red arrow). If you have a...[truncated 320 chars]...rim piece downwards and out while pulling it out from the sides. There are two small clips that sit in the vent (red arrow). Use care when pulling these out as they are small and can break.

Figure 4 Remove the trim piece from the top (red arrow).

Figure 5 Remove the odometer reset by pulling it straight out.

Figure 6 Use a long handled T20 Torx and remove the four Torx screws (red arrows) holding the cluster in the dash.

Figure 7 Over the years the sun and gravity will cause the dash to settle. Take your time and carefully remove the cluster from the dash by tilting it down from the top and then removing it.

Figure 8 There is a single electrical connection on the back of the cluster on the left side. Push in the tab on the back (red arrow), and slide the gray hinge upwards.

Figure 9 This will push the connector out from the cluster.

Figure 10 You can now remove the cluster from between the top of the steering wheel and dash. Installation is the reverse of removal."

It had a relatively new inspection sticker on it and no CEL when I bought it. Now it needs a New York inspection and I find out there are multiple codes and the CEL is not working.  
The car has always had an issue with the odometer reading 6 dashes instead of a real mileage. When I looked at it the first time it had a reading, but when I went back to complete the transaction, it was dashes. I figured that I would be able to fix it.  
The codes were crank position sensor and failure to reach operating temp (thermostat). I replaced both and cleared my codes. Shop says that it wont pass inspection with the CEL not working.

It is important to not let instrument cluster repairs go too long. If a cluster is failing then you will simply not know if there is damage to your engine, or if you are running low on oil. A broken instrument cluster can also make it difficult to sell your car. If you have a broken instrument cluster, the best thing to do is to take it to a professional for repair.

The best option is to work with ISS Automotive and get a replacement or repair.

[Click here to search for your instrument cluster >](/collections/instrument-cluster-repair)

### What's important when getting a repair or replacement?

It is important that you get the following with any repair or replacement:

- Lifetime Warranty
- Free Programming
- Parts that match your VIN
- Customer Service
- Technical Support
- Programming to your mileage
- Potentially upgrading to LED lights or other gauges

### How much does it cost to fix the instrument ...[truncated 8520 chars]...rs can be repaired, but the repair costs will probably be more than a new replacement part.

You can check first the wire harness. You need to make sure that all of the electrical contacts are clean and not corroded, then test them to see if power is getting from the board to the backlight.

Connect the harness to the backlight and then connect it to the board. Turn on the key in order to see if the backlight turns on with or without the engine running.

If the backlight doesn't turn on, it might be a problem with the circuit board or maybe even an issue with your battery.

If the backlight turns on when you test it, then there is a good chance that your board might just be corroded.

Instrument cluster repair costs will vary from shop to shop. In order for a professional to fully diagnose the problem, they will need your car's VIN number and all of the part numbers.

This will help them to determine what is damaged and then get an accurate estimate for a replacement part."

## Bottom line

The evidence supports **DIY removal and reinstall of your original/repaired cluster**, but **not casual full DIY replacement**. The physical job looks realistic: about 1 hour, trim tools plus Torx, four cluster fasteners, one rear connector. The risk is the electronics: VIN, mileage, EIS sync, tamper dot, and Mercedes coding.

## Removal sources compared

I used these source types:
- Exact GLK/X204 videos: MBOwnersClub GLK cluster video, Amazing Road TV X204 dash/cluster video, MBOwnersClub full GLK instrument panel video.
- Written guide: Pelican W204 cluster guide, closest compatible chassis.
- Forum/owner threads: BenzWorld X204 GLK cluster/coding thread and MBWorld GLK removal question.
- Parts diagram: Mercedes Classic/MBUSA 2012 GLK350 cluster and switches diagram.

Pelican’s W204 guide says the tools are a **trim removal tool** and **T20 Torx**, with the cluster held by **four Torx screws** and one rear electrical connector. <citation refs="6y2vM-ksqb_6O33sY0TEg">“Tools: Trim removal tool, T20 Torx”; “remove the four Torx screws”; “single electrical connection on the back.”</citation> The MBUSA parts page confirms the 2012 GLK350 instrument cluster as **204-900-46-07** in the cluster/switches group. <citation refs="6ysaO1R09ETuHrS_VzIOd">“Instrument Cluster” and “204-900-46-07.”</citation>

## Consolidated removal and reinstall steps

1. Park level, key out, steering wheel fully **down and pulled out**.
2. Consider disconnecting the battery if you are uncomfortable unplugging electronics.
